title: Harriet Higher Chair
summary: A wooden toddler high chair built from 2x2s with a solid seat and backrest. A few 10° angle cuts give the legs their splay and stability.
description:
A high chair has one job that matters above looks: it cannot tip. That's why the legs on this one splay outward, set by a handful of 10° cuts, giving a wider footprint at the floor than at the seat. Get those angles consistent and the chair is rock-solid; get them sloppy and it rocks.

The cut list is precise about which ends are angled and which are square, and about 'shortest point' versus 'parallel' measurements — read it carefully, because a 2x2 cut 10° at the wrong end puts the splay the wrong way. Set your saw to 10° once and cut all the like pieces together so they match. Build the two side assemblies first and make them mirror images; if they don't match exactly, the chair sits crooked.

Use 2-1/2" screws or pocket screws for the 2x2 joinery, but switch to 2" screws where you fasten up into the seat so nothing pokes through the surface a child sits on. The backrest height is worth dialing in with the actual child — the source builder sat their daughter in it to set the support. And because it's a high chair, plan to add a safety strap or harness: the wood holds the child up, the strap keeps them in.

tools.note: The 10° leg-splay cuts. Set it once and batch the matching pieces.
Every edge rounded — this is toddler furniture.
Used to set the backrest square and the chair sitting true.

materials.name: Child-safe finish
materials.note: A child eats at this chair — use a food-safe or non-toxic finish.

materials.name: Safety strap / harness
materials.note: Not woodworking, but required — a high chair needs a restraint.

cutList.note: One end 10° off square, shortest point.
One end 10° off square, shortest point.
Both ends 10° off square, parallel to each other.

steps.title: Build the two side assemblies
steps.body: Cut all the 10° pieces per the cut list, minding which ends are angled and whether the measurement is to the short point or parallel. Build the two side assemblies with 2-1/2" countersunk screws (or pocket screws) and glue, and make them mirror images. If the sides don't match, the finished chair rocks.

steps.title: Join the sides with the front apron
steps.body: Connect the two side assemblies with the front apron/support pieces, same fasteners and glue. Check the frame is square and sits flat before moving on.

steps.title: Attach the seat
steps.body: Set the 1x12 seat on the frame and fasten it — switch to 2" screws here so nothing pokes up through the seat surface. Screw the side aprons to the seat as well to lock it down.

steps.title: Build and attach the back
steps.body: Assemble the back from the two back supports and the back base with 2-1/2" fasteners, adjusting for square. Attach it to the seat assembly, then screw the backrest to the back supports.

steps.title: Fit the backrest, finish, and add a strap
steps.body: Set the backrest height to the actual child — sitting them in it is the honest way to get it right — level it and screw it home. Sand every edge round, finish with a food-safe product, and fit a safety strap. The wood holds the child up; the strap keeps them in.

images.alt: Wooden toddler high chair with splayed legs and a solid backrest
